title: Daily Plant Watering Calculator | 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
description: Calculate daily watering needs by plant species and climate. Free plant care calculator with expert formulas. Instant results.
intro: The Daily Plant Watering Calculator estimates how many liters (or gallons) of water a specific plant needs per day based on its species and current growth stage. It uses evapotranspiration principles combined with crop coefficients (Kc) established by the FAO to deliver science-backed irrigation values. Accurate daily watering is critical: overwatering drowns roots and promotes fungal disease, while underwatering causes wilting, blossom drop, and stunted growth. Use this calculator when setting up a drip irrigation schedule, troubleshooting plant stress, or planning water usage for a home garden or small farm.
keyTakeaway: **Daily Water Need (L/day) = ETo × Kc × Planting Area (m²)**, where ETo ≈ 5–7 mm/day in summer (US average) and Kc ranges from 0.3 (seedling) to 1.25 (peak fruiting). A single fruiting tomato plant in a 0.5 m² bed needs roughly 2.5–4 L/day.
useCases: Setting up a drip irrigation timer for a vegetable garden mid-summer to avoid daily manual guessing
Diagnosing whether a potted basil plant is receiving too little water during its active vegetative stage
Planning total weekly water consumption for a 20-plant raised-bed plot before a hot weather forecast
Adjusting watering frequency when transplanting seedlings from an indoor tray to an outdoor raised bed
Calculating water savings when switching from overhead sprinklers to targeted drip irrigation by species
explanation:
## How It Is Calculated

The calculator applies the **FAO-56 Penman-Monteith reference evapotranspiration model**, simplified for home use:

```
Daily Water Need (L/day) = ETo (mm/day) × Kc × A (m²) × 1 L/mm/m²
```

- **ETo** = Reference evapotranspiration (baseline water demand of a reference grass crop). US average: ~4 mm/day (cool season) to ~7 mm/day (peak summer). USDA and NOAA regional data refine this further.
- **Kc** = Crop coefficient — a dimensionless multiplier specific to the plant species and growth stage, published by FAO Irrigation Paper No. 56.
- **A** = Root zone surface area (m²) per plant or per planting bed.
- The result in liters assumes 1 mm of water over 1 m² = 1 liter.

For container plants, the formula adjusts to pot volume and drainage fraction:

```
Container Daily Need (L/day) = Pot Volume (L) × 0.5 × (ETo/5) × Kc
```

---

## Reference Table

| Plant Species | Growth Stage | Kc (FAO-56) | ETo Avg (mm/day) | Liters/Day per m² |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tomato | Seedling | 0.60 | 5.5 | 3.3 |
| Tomato | Vegetative | 0.85 | 5.5 | 4.7 |
| Tomato | Fruiting | 1.15 | 5.5 | **6.3** |
| Pepper | Vegetative | 0.70 | 5.5 | 3.9 |
| Pepper | Fruiting | 1.05 | 5.5 | 5.8 |
| Lettuce | All stages | 1.00 | 5.5 | 5.5 |
| Basil | Vegetative | 0.65 | 5.5 | 3.6 |
| Cucumber | Fruiting | 1.00 | 5.5 | 5.5 |
| Zucchini | Fruiting | 0.95 | 5.5 | 5.2 |
| Rose (outdoor) | Blooming | 0.85 | 5.5 | 4.7 |
| Succulent/Cactus | All stages | 0.10–0.15 | 5.5 | 0.6–0.8 |
| Lawn (fescue) | Active | 0.80 | 5.5 | 4.4 |

*Sources: FAO Irrigation Paper No. 56; USDA NRCS Irrigation Guide.*

---

## Typical Cases

### Case 1 — Fruiting Tomato in a Raised Bed
A home gardener in Phoenix, AZ (ETo ≈ 7 mm/day in July) has 4 tomato plants, each occupying ~0.5 m²:

```
Water/day = 7 mm × 1.15 × (4 × 0.5 m²) = 7 × 1.15 × 2 = 16.1 L/day total
≈ 4.0 L per plant per day
```

This aligns with the USDA recommendation of 1–2 inches of water per week during peak summer.

### Case 2 — Potted Basil on a Windowsill
A 3-liter pot, vegetative stage, indoors (ETo equivalent ≈ 3 mm/day due to lower evaporation):

```
Container Need = 3 L × 0.5 × (3/5) × 0.65 = 0.59 L/day ≈ 0.6 L/day
```

In practice, check the top 2 cm of soil daily — water when dry.

### Case 3 — Lettuce in a 4 m² Garden Bed
Cool spring conditions in Denver, CO (ETo ≈ 4 mm/day):

```
Water/day = 4 mm × 1.00 × 4 m² = 16 L/day for the entire bed
= 1.6 L per plant (10 plants)
```

Lettuce has shallow roots; split into two light waterings to prevent surface crust formation.

---

## Common Errors

1. **Ignoring growth stage changes**: Many gardeners use a single watering rate all season. Kc for tomato rises from 0.60 (seedling) to 1.15 (fruiting) — that's a ~92% increase in water demand that must be reflected in your schedule.

2. **Not accounting for rainfall**: If your area received 10 mm of rain yesterday (≈ 10 L/m²), subtract that from the daily need. Overwatering saturated soil pushes oxygen out and promotes Phytophthora root rot within 24–48 hours.

3. **Confusing pot capacity with daily need**: A 10-liter pot does NOT need 10 liters daily. Daily need is typically 30–50% of pot volume under peak summer conditions, and far less in shade or indoors.

4. **Using tap water volume as the watering benchmark**: Runoff and evaporation from overhead watering can mean only 50–70% of applied water actually reaches roots. Drip systems deliver 90–95% efficiency (USDA NRCS data). Adjust your total applied volume accordingly.

5. **Applying a flat "1 cup per day" rule across all species**: Succulents need ~0.1× the water of fruiting vegetables. A rule of thumb sized for tomatoes will kill a cactus within weeks via root rot.

6. **Ignoring soil type**: Sandy soils drain 3–5× faster than clay. In sandy soil, split daily need into 2–3 applications; in clay-heavy soil, apply slowly to prevent runoff and compaction.

faq.q: How many liters of water does a tomato plant need per day?
faq.a: A fruiting tomato plant in a 0.5 m² bed requires approximately 2.5–4.5 liters per day during peak summer (June–August in most of the US). This is based on a Kc of 1.15 and an average ETo of 5–7 mm/day. In hot, dry climates like Arizona, this can reach 5–6 L/day per plant. Seedlings need roughly half that amount.

faq.q: What is a crop coefficient (Kc) and why does it matter?
faq.a: The crop coefficient (Kc) is a dimensionless multiplier published by the FAO (Irrigation Paper No. 56) that adjusts reference evapotranspiration (ETo) for a specific plant and its growth stage. For example, tomatoes have Kc = 0.60 at seedling stage and 1.15 at peak fruiting. Without applying the correct Kc, your watering estimate can be off by 50–90%.

faq.q: How often should I water succulents and cacti?
faq.a: Succulents and cacti have a Kc of 0.10–0.15, meaning they need roughly 10–15% of the water a standard vegetable crop does. Under typical indoor conditions, watering every 10–14 days in summer and every 3–4 weeks in winter is sufficient. Overwatering is the #1 cause of succulent death — root rot can set in within 48 hours of waterlogged soil.

faq.q: Does rainfall count toward my plants' daily water needs?
faq.a: Yes. If your area receives measurable rainfall (≥3 mm), subtract that from your calculated daily need. The USDA Natural Resources Conservation Service recommends tracking weekly precipitation and adjusting irrigation accordingly. A rain gauge is a $5–$10 investment that can cut summer water bills by 15–30%.

faq.q: Is it better to water in the morning or evening?
faq.a: The USDA and most cooperative extension services recommend early morning watering (6–9 AM). Foliage has time to dry before nightfall, reducing the risk of fungal diseases like powdery mildew and botrytis by up to 50%. Evening watering leaves leaves wet overnight, creating ideal conditions for pathogen growth. Midday watering wastes up to 30% of water to evaporation.

faq.q: How does growth stage affect daily water needs?
faq.a: Growth stage is one of the two biggest factors in plant water demand. For tomatoes: seedling stage (Kc 0.60) → vegetative (Kc 0.85) → flowering (Kc 1.05) → fruiting (Kc 1.15) → late ripening (Kc 0.80). This means a fully fruiting plant needs ~92% more water than a seedling of the same species. Failing to adjust for stage is the most common reason for mid-season plant stress.

faq.q: How do I calculate water needs for a container (potted) plant?
faq.a: For containers, use: Daily Need (L) = Pot Volume (L) × 0.5 × (ETo/5) × Kc. A 5-liter pot with a vegetative pepper (Kc 0.70) in average summer conditions (ETo 5 mm/day): 5 × 0.5 × 1.0 × 0.70 = 1.75 L/day. Always check drainage — if water pools on top for >30 seconds, the soil is saturated and you should skip that day's watering.

faq.q: What is ETo and how do I find my local value?
faq.a: ETo (Reference Evapotranspiration) measures how much water a standard reference crop (clipped grass) loses to evaporation and transpiration daily, expressed in mm/day. US averages range from ~2 mm/day in winter to ~7+ mm/day in peak summer. Your local ETo data is freely available from NOAA Climate Data Online and the USDA National Agricultural Statistics Service by zip code or county.

faq.q: How much water does a vegetable garden use per week in summer?
faq.a: The standard recommendation from USDA extension services is 1–2 inches of water per week (25–50 mm/week) for most vegetable gardens, which translates to 25–50 liters per m² per week. A 4 m² raised bed with mixed vegetables needs approximately 100–200 liters per week in peak summer. Drip irrigation can reduce this by 30–50% compared to overhead sprinklers due to higher delivery efficiency.

sources.name: FAO Irrigation and Drainage Paper No. 56 — Crop Evapotranspiration (ETo & Kc values)

NOAA Climate Data Online — Evapotranspiration & Weather Data by Location

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, no external data updates required
